title: How C Works ⚙️
description: Understand the magic behind the C compiler.

slides.title: The Language Barrier 💬
slides.content:
Computers are actually quite dumb. They only understand **Machine Code**, which is just a bunch of 1s and 0s (binary).

If you type `#include <stdio.h>`, the computer has *no idea* what you're talking about. We need a translator!

slides.title: Meet the Compiler 🏗️
slides.content:
C is a **compiled language**.

A special program called a **Compiler** reads your readable C code (the *source code*) and translates it entirely into machine code *before* the program runs.

The most famous C compiler is called **GCC** (GNU Compiler Collection).

quizData.question: What is the job of the C compiler?

options.text: To print text to the screen.
To fix logic errors in your code.
To translate human-readable C code into machine code.

quizData.explanation: The compiler acts as a translator between you and the CPU.

slides.title: The Compilation Steps 🚶
slides.content:
The compilation process actually happens in 4 steps:

1. **Preprocessing**: The `#include` statements are fulfilled.
2. **Compiling**: The code is converted into Assembly language.
3. **Assembling**: Assembly is converted into pure binary object files.
4. **Linking**: Object files and libraries (like `stdio.h`) are linked together into one final Executable (.exe on Windows, .out on Mac/Linux).

quizData.question: In which step are the #include libraries added to the program?
quizData.explanation: The preprocessor handles all lines starting with the '#' symbol.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## Compiled vs Interpreted

Some languages like Python are *interpreted* (translated line-by-line as they run). C is *compiled* (translated all at once beforehand). This makes C slightly harder to test instantly, but **vastly faster** when running!
